title: A Future Like This Exists, Too
description: A glimpse into a possible future—Adachi and Shimamura as parents, with two daughters and Yashiro, sharing a quiet moment at a donut shop.
content:
I lifted the donut I'd just bought and peeked through the hole at Adachi. She looked back through her own, and the moment our eyes met, we both burst into laughter.

'Ah! Mommy, look—look!' Our older daughter hurried over, held up her half-eaten donut, and mimicked us by peering through the hole. We'd stopped by a donut shop during a family outing, and somehow our moods felt rounder and sweeter than the donuts themselves.

Our older daughter was the energetic type; our younger one, quiet and gentle. The little one looked up at me with a troubled expression. She had picked an angel-cream donut—the kind without a hole. Just as she puffed up her cheeks for a pouty 'Mmmph…' I froze, panicking inside.

Adachi, of course, stayed calm. She simply swapped donuts with her. Our younger daughter's pout melted into a smile, and she peeked up at me through the donut hole.

'Hi there.'

Seeing her peaceful little face framed by the donut, I finally relaxed.

'Smooth move, Mom.'

'Huh? Really…?' Adachi smiled—soft, a little shy, yet touched with a new kind of maturity that only made her more beautiful. Watching her grow into this person, year after year, made something warm and proud rise quietly in my chest.

Our older daughter munched happily and spoke with a grin.

'Heehee, Adacchi, are you okay with that donut?'

'I am. But you should call me Mom.'

'But if I call you both Mom, I won't know who I'm talking to.'

…Fair enough. I imagined the two of us turning around every time someone yelled 'Mom!' and nearly laughed.

'In that case, call her Mama Adachi.'

'Mhm! Or shorter—Adacchi!'

'Ugh…'

It seemed she had no escape. Adachi winced as our daughter pressed the nickname onto her like a victory stamp.

I raised my donut again for another bite—but suddenly, a new figure appeared inside the donut-hole view. A small tanuki girl had snuck up beside our daughters, her fluffy tail swaying.

'Something's there,' I murmured.

'Hohoho,' Yashiro cackled.

'Mmm, Yaichi' Our older daughter poked her cheeks. The younger one gently tugged her wagging tail. She visited our home so often that she and the girls had become close without us noticing. Our younger daughter especially adored Yashiro's pale-blue hair and styled it every chance she got.

The little one broke her donut in half and held out a piece.

'Here. For you.'

'Ooh. My gratitude.' Yashiro took a huge bite out of it, completely unreserved. The younger girl smiled softly. She doesn't speak much, a quiet and well-behaved child, but she's endlessly gentle with friends—an affectionate little thing who loves clinging to her moms.

Our older daughter, meanwhile, is so energetic it's almost worrying. Keeping up with her takes effort—double when she and Yashiro start running around together—but she's just as lovable.

As she ate, Yashiro grinned. 'Mama and the others are waiting for you.'

'Oh—right. Let's head over once we're done. If we make Grandma wait too long, she'll scold us.'

Not that she wouldn't scold us even if we were on time.

We finished our donuts, washed our hands, and then Adachi and I each took one daughter's hand, holding them tight. As always, Yashiro climbed up and lay across the top of my head like it was her designated perch.

'Aaah, Yashiro gets to do that… Mom, I want too!'

'Nope. Only one person is allowed up here. But you can have this instead.'

I swung our older daughter's hand high, making her squeal with delight.

'Mama Adachi, aren't you gonna hold my hand too?'

I laughed and glanced at Adachi. With quiet grace, she smiled back.

'Of course I am.'

Naturally, we took each other's free hand. And together, our little family began walking forward.

The slanting sunlight brushed one side of Adachi's cheek in warm gold. Bathed in that gentle light, I let the truth slip out.

'I keep finding myself wishing… every day, every second… that moments like this could last forever.'

Words that once stayed buried rose like a prayer.

And as if the world were answering that prayer, countless pale-blue lights shimmered into existence—drifting softly through the air around us.
